title: Epistemology & Theory of Knowledge
subtitle: What we can know, how we know it, and the limits of certainty
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark argument, thought experiment, paper or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags reception, major objections, and ongoing debates. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 the argument or finding is widely accepted as establishing its conclusion · 🟡 contested or substantially qualified · 🔴 the argument is generally considered to fail. Map pins mark the institution where the key work was produced.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise what knowledge is, how it is justified, and what truth requires

entries.label: Justified True Belief (JTB)
entries.hint: The classical analysis holds that a person knows that P if and only if P is true, the person believes P, and the person is justified in believing P. This tripartite definition — True + Believed + Justified — organised epistemology for more than two millennia, from Plato's Theaetetus through twentieth-century analytic philosophy. Edmund Gettier's 1963 paper showed in three pages that all three conditions can be satisfied while knowledge is absent.
entries.note: Plato. Theaetetus (~369 BCE), 200d-210b, where Socrates explores knowledge as 'true belief with a logos.' The tripartite formulation became explicit in twentieth-century analytic epistemology: Ayer, A. J. (1956). The Problem of Knowledge. Macmillan; Chisholm, R. (1966). Theory of Knowledge. Prentice-Hall. The formulation was the working consensus when Gettier, E. L. (1963). 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' Analysis, 23(6), 121-123, published three counterexamples in three pages. No fully satisfactory fourth condition has been agreed since; the knowledge-first programme argues the analysis project is misguided in principle.

entries.label: Knowledge-First Epistemology
entries.hint: Timothy Williamson's Knowledge and Its Limits (2000) inverted the traditional project. Rather than analysing knowledge as JTB-plus-something, Williamson argues that knowledge is the most fundamental factive mental state — irreducible and therefore the right starting point for epistemology, not its conclusion. On this view asking 'what must be added to belief to get knowledge?' is the wrong question. Evidence is what you know (E=K), and knowledge is the norm of assertion.
entries.note: Williamson, T. (2000). Knowledge and Its Limits. Oxford University Press. E=K thesis: your total evidence is precisely what you know. Knowledge as the norm of assertion: assert P only if you know P. Main opponents: Conee, E. & Feldman, R. (2004). Evidentialism. Oxford University Press — belief-based analysis is viable. Ichikawa, J. J. & Steup, M. (2018). 'The Analysis of Knowledge.' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy surveys responses. The programme has generated substantial literature on safe belief, sensitive conditionals, and the relationship between knowledge and action.

entries.label: Correspondence Theory of Truth
entries.loc: Trinity College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: A proposition is true if and only if it corresponds to a fact about the world. Aristotle's formulation — 'to say of what is that it is, and of what is not that it is not, is true' — underpins the commonsense view; Russell and Wittgenstein gave it modern logical form. It is the default assumption behind the T in the JTB analysis. The main challenges are specifying what 'correspondence' and 'fact' mean precisely enough to do philosophical work without circularity.
entries.note: Aristotle. Metaphysics, 1011b25. Russell, B. (1912). The Problems of Philosophy. Williams & Norgate, ch. 12 — the modern analytic formulation. Wittgenstein, L. (1921). Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus — the picture theory as a correspondence account. Austin-Strawson debate: Austin, J. L. & Strawson, P. F. (1950). 'Symposium: Truth.' Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society, Supp. 24, 111-172 — challenged whether correspondence is a genuine relation. Armstrong, D. M. (2004). Truth and Truthmakers. Cambridge University Press is the contemporary successor specifying correspondence relata as truthmakers.

entries.label: Coherence Theory of Truth
entries.loc: Merton College, University of Oxford, Oxford, England
entries.hint: A proposition is true in virtue of cohering with a system of other propositions — not because it corresponds to a mind-independent reality. Truth is a property of whole systems of belief, not individual sentences. Developed in the idealist tradition by Bradley and Blanshard. The isolation objection is its principal weakness: any internally consistent fiction would qualify as true. Important note: coherence as a theory of truth is distinct from coherentism as a theory of justification, though both trade on the same intuition.
entries.note: Bradley, F. H. (1893). Appearance and Reality. Swan Sonnenschein — the foundational idealist statement. Blanshard, B. (1939). The Nature of Thought. Allen & Unwin, vol. 2, chs 25-27 — most systematic development. The isolation objection: Russell, B. (1906) and Rescher, N. (1973). The Coherence Theory of Truth. Oxford University Press. The theory survives in anti-realist philosophy of science and constructivist accounts of mathematical truth; as a general theory of truth it is largely abandoned outside neo-idealist circles. Do not conflate with th8 (coherentism about justification), which is a theory about a different question entirely.

entries.label: Pragmatic Theory of Truth
entries.hint: A proposition is true if believing it 'works' — if it leads to successful action and proves itself in experience. William James proposed that truth is not a static relation between sentence and fact but a process: an idea becomes true insofar as it helps us get into satisfactory relations with experience. Pragmatist truth collapses the gap between 'is true' and 'is warranted to assert,' which critics take to be a fatal confusion — a useful belief may still be false.
entries.note: James, W. (1907). Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking. Longmans, Green, Lecture VI. Peirce, C. S. (1877). 'The Fixation of Belief.' Popular Science Monthly — earlier version: truth is the limit of ideal scientific inquiry. Dewey, J. (1938). Logic: The Theory of Inquiry — extends pragmatism to formal logic. Russell's objection: 'will to believe' problem — a belief can be useful and false simultaneously, so utility cannot constitute truth. Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History develops 'internal realism,' a neo-pragmatist position attempting to avoid relativism. Rorty's (1979) eliminative pragmatism (truth is merely a compliment paid to well-warranted assertions) is the most radical contemporary descendant.

entries.label: Deflationary Theory of Truth
entries.loc: King's College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: Saying 'Snow is white is true' adds nothing to saying 'Snow is white.' Truth is not a substantive property but a logical device for disquotation and generalisation. Ramsey proposed this in 1927; later variants include Quine's disquotational theory, the prosentential theory (Grover), and Horwich's minimalism. Deflationism avoids all the problems of correspondence and coherence theories by denying there is a deep metaphysical property for them to describe.
entries.note: Ramsey, F. P. (1927). 'Facts and Propositions.' Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society, Supp. Vol. 7, 153-170 — the founding statement. Quine, W. V. O. (1970). Philosophy of Logic. Harvard University Press, ch. 1 — disquotational formulation. Horwich, P. (1990). Truth. Blackwell — minimalism: the whole theory of truth is the set of instances of the schema 'P is true iff P.' Main challenge: deflationism struggles to explain why truth is the goal of inquiry or the norm of assertion if it is merely a logical device. Stoljar, D. & Damnjanovic, N. (2014). 'The Deflationary Theory of Truth.' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy surveys the variants and objections.

entries.label: Foundationalism
entries.hint: Epistemic justification has a foundation: some beliefs are basic — justified without deriving justification from other beliefs — and all non-basic beliefs are justified by inference from these. Descartes sought incorrigible foundations (the cogito); modern fallibilist foundationalism accepts that basic beliefs can be defeasible. The position is motivated by Agrippa's regress: if every belief requires justification by a prior belief, either the chain is infinite, circular, or stops at some basic belief. Foundationalism takes the third option.
entries.note: Descartes, R. (1641). Meditationes de Prima Philosophia — the Cartesian programme with incorrigible foundations. Contemporary statements: Chisholm, R. (1966). Theory of Knowledge. Prentice-Hall; Alston, W. P. (1989). Epistemic Justification. Cornell University Press. Fallibilist foundationalism (the dominant contemporary form): Pryor, J. (2000). 'The Sceptic and the Dogmatist.' Nous, 34, 517-549. The regress argument originates in Aristotle, Posterior Analytics I.3, and is formalised as Agrippa's Trilemma (see ef1). Main competitor: th8 (coherentism), which accepts circularity as benign provided the circle is large and comprehensive enough.

entries.label: Coherentism about Justification
entries.hint: A belief is justified if it coheres well with the rest of one's belief system — there is no privileged foundation; every belief is in principle revisable in light of the others. Neurath's image: we are sailors repairing a ship while at sea, replacing planks one at a time without ever putting it in dry dock. A belief's justification comes from mutual support with neighbouring beliefs, not derivation from a secure base. The isolation objection — that any consistent fiction would be internally coherent — is the main challenge.
entries.note: Neurath, O. (1932/33). 'Protokollsatze.' Erkenntnis, 3, 204-214 — the boat metaphor. BonJour, L. (1985). The Structure of Empirical Knowledge. Harvard University Press — the fullest defence. Isolation objection: Rescher, N. (1973). The Coherence Theory of Truth. Oxford University Press. BonJour later abandoned coherentism: BonJour, L. (1998). In Defense of Pure Reason. Cambridge University Press — an unusually public recantation. Quine's web-of-belief holism (Two Dogmas, 1951 — st4) is a coherentist-adjacent picture: statements face the tribunal of experience collectively, not individually. Distinct from th4 (coherence theory of truth), which is about what truth consists in, not about what justifies belief.

entries.label: Reliabilism
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: A belief is justified if it was produced by a reliable cognitive process — one that tends to produce true beliefs. Goldman's process reliabilism shifts focus from the internal reasons a believer can access (internalism) to the external fact of whether the process works. Perception, memory, and careful inference are reliable; wishful thinking and astrology are not. This makes justification an objective, external matter rather than a function of the agent's internal perspective on their own reasons.
entries.note: Goldman, A. I. (1979). 'What Is Justified Belief?' In Pappas, G. (Ed.), Justification and Knowledge. Reidel, 1-23 — the founding paper. Extended in Goldman, A. I. (1986). Epistemology and Cognition. Harvard University Press. The generality problem: which process type determines reliability? Conee, E. & Feldman, R. (1998). 'The Generality Problem for Reliabilism.' Philosophical Studies, 89, 1-29. BonJour's clairvoyant case is the most discussed counterexample. Virtue reliabilism is a descendant that individuates processes through intellectual virtues, partially addressing the generality problem.

entries.label: Virtue Epistemology
entries.hint: Epistemological evaluation should centre on the intellectual virtues of the believer, not just the properties of individual beliefs. Sosa distinguishes animal knowledge (belief that manifests a reliable faculty) from reflective knowledge (the believer can also account for why the faculty is reliable). Zagzebski stresses character virtues: open-mindedness, intellectual humility, thoroughness. The approach resolves Gettier cases by requiring that knowledge be a cognitive achievement that is properly creditable to the agent's competence — lucky true belief does not qualify.
entries.note: Sosa, E. (1980). 'The Raft and the Pyramid.' Midwest Studies in Philosophy, 5, 3-25 — founding paper. Sosa, E. (1991). Knowledge in Perspective. Cambridge University Press. Zagzebski, L. (1996). Virtues of the Mind. Cambridge University Press — the responsibilist programme centred on character virtues. Greco, J. (2010). Achieving Knowledge. Cambridge University Press — resolves Gettier: knowledge = true belief that is a manifestation of intellectual virtue. Challenge: Lackey, J. (2007). 'Why We Don't Deserve Credit for Everything We Know.' Synthese, 158, 345-361 — testimonial knowledge can be justified even when the agent deserves little credit for it, straining the achievement model.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining arguments — and exactly what they established

entries.label: Gettier Counterexamples
entries.hint: In three pages Edmund Gettier demolished the JTB analysis that had organised epistemology for two millennia. His counterexamples show that justified true belief is insufficient for knowledge: Smith has good reason to believe Jones will get the job, and infers 'the person who gets the job has ten coins in their pocket.' Smith himself gets the job and happens to have ten coins — the belief is justified and true, but the source of justification is disconnected from what makes it true. The paper triggered fifty years of analysis without consensus.
entries.note: Gettier, E. L. (1963). 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' Analysis, 23(6), 121-123. Two and a half pages; the most-cited short paper in the history of philosophy. Responses fall into four families: (1) add a fourth condition — defeasibility (Lehrer & Paxson 1969), no false lemmas (Clark 1963), sensitivity (Nozick 1981); (2) replace JTB with a different analysis — reliabilism, virtue epistemology; (3) abandon the analysis project — knowledge-first; (4) refine justification. No consensus fourth condition has survived. Zagzebski, L. (1994). 'The Inescapability of Gettier Problems.' Philosophical Quarterly, 44, 65-73 argues any JTB+X analysis is vulnerable to Gettier-style construction.

entries.label: Descartes' Meditations on First Philosophy
entries.hint: The founding text of modern epistemology. Descartes employs methodological doubt: set aside whatever can be doubted, however improbable the doubt. He doubts the senses (they sometimes deceive), then all perception (the dream argument — perhaps all experience is a dream), then mathematics (an omnipotent evil demon might induce systematic error even in clear-and-distinct reasoning). From this abyss he extracts the cogito — 'I think, therefore I am' — as the one incorrigible certainty, then rebuilds knowledge on it via God's non-deception.
entries.note: Descartes, R. (1641). Meditationes de Prima Philosophia. Michel Soly, Paris. Standard translation: Cottingham, J., Stoothoff, R. & Murdoch, D. (Eds.) (1984). The Philosophical Writings of Descartes, vol. 2. Cambridge University Press. The evil demon (Meditatio I) is the template for all subsequent sceptical scenarios including BIV. The Cartesian Circle controversy: the cogito's certainty appears to depend on God's veracity, but God's existence is proved by reasoning whose reliability presupposes certainty. Wilson, M. D. (1978). Descartes. Routledge is the standard secondary reference.

entries.label: Hume's Problem of Induction
entries.hint: We cannot rationally justify inferring from past observations to future events. All inductive inference assumes the uniformity of nature — that the future resembles the past — but this principle cannot itself be justified without circularity (using induction to justify induction) or a priori reasoning (it is not a logical necessity). Hume's problem is not a sceptical curiosity: it strikes at the foundations of all empirical science. He concluded that induction is a habit of mind, not a rational inference — the most deflationary possible resolution.
entries.note: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature, Book I, Part III — first statement. Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding, Section IV — the canonical formulation. Goodman, N. (1955). Fact, Fiction, and Forecast. Harvard University Press — the 'new riddle of induction' (grue paradox): even granting the uniformity of nature, we cannot determine which predicates project correctly. Popper, K. (1934/1959). The Logic of Scientific Discovery proposes falsificationism as an escape: science avoids induction by conjecture and refutation. Bayesian epistemology offers probabilistic confirmation theory as a partial answer; the problem of the prior remains.

entries.label: Quine's "Two Dogmas of Empiricism"
entries.hint: Quine attacked the two central dogmas of logical empiricism: (1) the analytic/synthetic distinction — that some truths hold purely in virtue of meaning — and (2) reductionism — that each statement can be individually confirmed by experience. He argued that the whole web of belief faces the tribunal of experience together; any statement can be held true by adjusting others, including logic and mathematics. This holism dissolved the sharp boundary between empirical science and philosophy, and demolished logical positivism as a programme.
entries.note: Quine, W. V. O. (1951). 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism.' Philosophical Review, 60(1), 20-43. One of the most influential papers in 20th-century philosophy. Responses: Grice, H. P. & Strawson, P. F. (1956). 'In Defense of a Dogma.' Philosophical Review, 65, 141-158 — defended the analytic/synthetic distinction. Boghossian, P. (1996). 'Analyticity Reconsidered.' Nous, 30, 360-391 — revived the distinction. Repl mixed: the holism is widely accepted; the rejection of analyticity remains contested. The web-of-belief picture anticipates coherentism; the paper led directly to naturalised epistemology.

entries.label: Quine's "Epistemology Naturalized"
entries.hint: Quine proposed abandoning the traditional project of rational reconstruction — the attempt to validate science from a prior, foundational standpoint. Since Carnap's programme failed, epistemology should become a chapter of cognitive psychology: we study empirically how humans form beliefs from sensory input rather than trying to justify those processes from the outside. The normative ambitions of traditional epistemology are to be replaced by descriptive natural science. This generated one of the most sustained debates in contemporary philosophy: can naturalism preserve normativity, or does it explain it away?
entries.note: Quine, W. V. O. (1969). 'Epistemology Naturalized.' In Ontological Relativity and Other Essays. Columbia University Press, 69-90. Circularity objection: natural science cannot validate the cognitive processes that generate science without vicious circularity. Kim, J. (1988). 'What Is Naturalized Epistemology?' Philosophical Perspectives, 2, 381-405 — the canonical objection: naturalism cannot replace normative epistemology. Responses: Goldman's reliabilism is a naturalised programme that preserves normativity through the notion of a reliable process. Kornblith, H. (2002). Knowledge and Its Place in Nature. Oxford University Press — thoroughgoing naturalist response.

entries.label: Kuhn's Structure of Scientific Revolutions
entries.hint: Science does not progress by the gradual accumulation of truths; it moves through 'paradigm shifts' — revolutionary episodes in which one framework is replaced by another. In normal science, researchers solve puzzles within a paradigm without questioning its foundations. Anomalies accumulate until crisis triggers a revolution. Kuhn's concept of incommensurability — that scientists in rival paradigms cannot straightforwardly compare theories because they perceive the world differently — threatened the standard picture of science as rational and objective, generating enormous controversy.
entries.note: Kuhn, T. S. (1962). The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. University of Chicago Press. Second edition (1970) adds a Postscript defending incommensurability. Cited over one million times by 2012 — one of the most influential academic books of the 20th century. Lakatos, I. (1970). 'Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes' develops a rival rational-reconstruction account without incommensurability. Feyerabend, P. (1975). Against Method radicalises Kuhn's relativistic implications. Kuhn resisted relativist readings; Bird, A. (2000). Thomas Kuhn. Princeton University Press reconstructs a realist-compatible interpretation.

entries.label: Putnam's Brain-in-a-Vat Argument
entries.hint: Imagine your brain has been removed and connected to a supercomputer that feeds it exactly the sensory inputs you would normally receive. You would believe you had a body, lived in a world — but all your beliefs would be false. Putnam argues this scenario is self-refuting: if 'brain in a vat' were true, the phrase 'brain in a vat' would refer to computer-simulation inputs, not actual brains and vats, because meaning is partly constituted by causal relations to the environment. The sceptical hypothesis undermines itself through semantic externalism.
entries.note: Putnam, H. (1981). Reason, Truth and History. Cambridge University Press, ch. 1. The argument exploits semantic externalism from Putnam, H. (1975). 'The Meaning of Meaning.' Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science, 7, 131-193 — meanings are not in the head; they depend on causal-environmental relations. The self-refutation response is contested: Brueckner, A. (1986). 'Brains in a Vat.' Journal of Philosophy, 83, 148-167 argues it depends on controversial semantic assumptions. Contextualism offers an alternative response that avoids the semantic machinery. The BIV scenario remains the standard formulation of Cartesian scepticism in contemporary analytic epistemology.

entries.label: BonJour's Clairvoyant Case
entries.hint: Norman is a reliable clairvoyant: his faculty produces true beliefs about the President's location without any evidence accessible to Norman himself. Process reliabilism says Norman's belief is justified because the process is reliable. BonJour argues it obviously is not — Norman has no reason to trust his faculty and has not exercised any epistemic responsibility. The case motivates internalism (justification requires accessible reasons) and virtue epistemology's requirement that knowledge be a recognisable achievement traceable to the agent.
entries.note: BonJour, L. (1980). 'Externalist Theories of Empirical Knowledge.' Midwest Studies in Philosophy, 5, 53-73. The canonical counterexample to process reliabilism. Goldman, A. I. (1986). Epistemology and Cognition responds by tightening the specification of process types. Sosa, E. (1991) reframes the debate: animal knowledge (reliable process) vs. reflective knowledge (the believer understands why the faculty is reliable) — the latter addresses BonJour's worry. BonJour later abandoned his coherentist alternative: BonJour, L. (1998). In Defense of Pure Reason. Cambridge University Press — one of the more public theoretical reversals in contemporary epistemology.

entries.label: DeRose's Contextualism
entries.hint: The truth conditions of knowledge attributions vary with the conversational context of the attributer. In ordinary contexts, 'I know there is a hand before me' is true because sceptical alternatives are not salient. In a philosophical discussion where brain-in-a-vat scenarios are raised, the same sentence becomes false — not because my epistemic position has changed, but because 'know' has raised its standards in the high-stakes context. Contextualism thereby validates both everyday knowledge claims and the sceptic's conclusions without contradiction.
entries.note: DeRose, K. (1992). 'Contextualism and Knowledge Attributions.' Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 52(4), 913-929. Lewis, D. (1996). 'Elusive Knowledge.' Australasian Journal of Philosophy, 74, 549-567 — parallel development: 'know' is context-sensitive like 'flat' or 'empty.' Main opponent: Stanley, J. (2005). Knowledge and Practical Interests. Oxford University Press — subject-sensitive invariantism: standards vary with the subject's stakes, not the attributer's context. Hawthorne, J. (2004). Knowledge and Lotteries argues contextualism has unacceptable semantic consequences. DeRose, K. (2009). The Case for Contextualism. Oxford University Press is the book-length defence.

entries.label: Al-Ghazali's Deliverance from Error
entries.hint: Five centuries before Descartes, al-Ghazali underwent systematic methodological doubt at the height of his career. He doubted the senses (stars appear small; they are larger than the earth), then doubted reason itself (perhaps a higher state would reveal rational certainties to be as illusory as dream certainties). He arrived at complete epistemological collapse before recovering certainty through divine illumination. His parallel critique of Aristotelian causality — fire does not cause burning; God creates the conjunction — anticipates Hume's analysis by six hundred years.
entries.note: Al-Ghazali (c. 1095). Deliverance from Error (Al-Munqidh min al-Dalal). Translation: McCarthy, R. J. (1980). Freedom and Fulfillment. Twayne Publishers. The critique of causality: Al-Ghazali (1095). The Incoherence of the Philosophers (Tahafut al-Falasifa), 17th discussion. Ibn Rushd responded in The Incoherence of the Incoherence (Tahafut al-Tahafut, c. 1180). Griffel, F. (2009). Al-Ghazali's Philosophical Theology. Oxford University Press reconstructs the epistemological argument. Leaman, O. (2002) notes the structural parallel with Descartes and Hume but cautions against over-assimilation: al-Ghazali's resolution through divine illumination (nur) has no secular Cartesian analogue.

entries.label: Fricker's Epistemic Injustice
entries.loc: Birkbeck, University of London, London, England
entries.hint: Miranda Fricker identified two forms of epistemic injustice — wrongs done to someone specifically in their capacity as a knower. Testimonial injustice: a speaker receives less credibility than deserved because of prejudice about their social identity. Hermeneutical injustice: a speaker lacks the conceptual resources to understand their own experience because marginalised groups are excluded from the collective meaning-making process (the absence of a word like 'sexual harassment' before it was coined). Epistemic injustice is simultaneously an epistemic failure and an ethical wrong.
entries.note: Fricker, M. (2007). Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethical Life of Knowledge. Oxford University Press. One of the fastest-growing literatures in contemporary philosophy. Extensions: Dotson, K. (2011). 'Tracking Epistemic Violence, Tracking Practices of Silencing.' Hypatia, 26(2), 236-257 — epistemic violence. Medina, J. (2013). The Epistemology of Resistance. Oxford University Press. Critique: Kotzee, B. (2011) argues testimonial injustice conflates credibility deficits with moral injustice. Hookway, C. (2010). 'Some Varieties of Epistemic Injustice.' Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society, 110, 247-264 proposes a three-part taxonomy. The concept has influenced law, medicine, and clinical epistemology.

entries.label: G.E. Moore: "Here is One Hand"
entries.hint: Moore's 'Proof of an External World' (1939) presents the most famous common-sense response to Cartesian scepticism. Holding up his hands, Moore declares: 'Here is one hand — and here is another. I know these are hands. Therefore the external world exists.' The Moorean shift exploits a logical asymmetry. The sceptic's argument runs: if I have hands then I am not a brain in a vat; I don't know I'm not in a vat; therefore I don't know I have hands. Moore reverses this modus ponens into modus tollens: I do know I have hands; if I have hands I'm not in a vat; therefore I am not in a vat. The question of which direction to run the argument cannot be settled by the argument itself, but Moore insists that ordinary perceptual knowledge provides stronger grounds for its own reliability than any philosophical argument for scepticism does for doubting it.
entries.note: Moore, G. E. (1939). 'Proof of an External World.' Proceedings of the British Academy, 25, 273-300 — delivered as the British Academy lecture. Moore, G. E. (1925). 'A Defence of Common Sense.' In Muirhead, J. H. (Ed.), Contemporary British Philosophy (2nd series). Allen & Unwin, 191-224 — companion manifesto listing certainties Moore claims to know. Wittgenstein, L. (1969). On Certainty. Blackwell — the most important response: some 'hinge' propositions anchor a practice of enquiry and cannot be meaningfully doubted within it; Moore was right that he knew, but wrong that he could prove it. Pryor, J. (2000). 'The Skeptic and the Dogmatist.' Nous, 34(4), 517-549 — 'dogmatism': immediate perceptual experience gives prima facie justification for perceptual beliefs, vindicating the Moorean move. Stroud, B. (1984). The Significance of Philosophical Scepticism. Oxford University Press, ch. 3 — the most compelling sceptical rejoinder: the Moorean move begs the question by assuming the very thing in dispute. Wright, C. (2004). 'Warrant for Nothing (and Foundations for Free)?' Aristotelian Society Supplementary Volume, 78(1), 167-212 — 'entitlement' theory reconstructs the Moorean move as rational acceptance without evidence.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, replicable problems and phenomena in epistemological inquiry

entries.label: Agrippa's Trilemma (The Regress Problem)
entries.hint: Any attempt to justify a belief leads to one of three equally unacceptable outcomes: an infinite regress of justifications (each belief requires another), a vicious circle (the chain loops back to the original belief), or a dogmatic stop (justification simply halts at some unjustified belief). Agrippa identified these three modes in ancient Pyrrhonism; the trilemma is the structural problem that foundationalism, coherentism, and infinitism each attempt to resolve differently. No response commands consensus.
entries.note: Attributed to Agrippa, a Pyrrhonist sceptic, via Sextus Empiricus (c. 200 CE). Outlines of Pyrrhonism, I.164-169. Klein, P. (1999). 'Human Knowledge and the Infinite Regress of Reasons.' Philosophical Perspectives, 13, 297-325 defends infinitism as a viable fourth response: the chain can be infinite without being vicious provided the believer can always produce a further reason. Turri, J. & Klein, P. (Eds.) (2014). Ad Infinitum: New Essays on Epistemological Infinitism. Oxford University Press surveys the contemporary debate. Repl robust: the trilemma structure is not contested; the adequacy of each proposed escape is.

entries.label: Epistemic Luck
entries.hint: Gettier cases are cases of epistemic luck: the belief is true, but only by luck — the source of justification is disconnected from what makes the belief true. Veritic luck is the general phenomenon; safe belief is its converse — a belief is safe if it could not easily have been false in similar circumstances. Epistemic luck is the bridge concept between the Gettier problem and virtue epistemology: knowledge requires not just justified true belief but true belief that is not merely luckily true.
entries.note: Pritchard, D. (2005). Epistemic Luck. Oxford University Press — the systematic treatment. Earlier: Unger, P. (1968). 'An Analysis of Factual Knowledge.' Journal of Philosophy, 65, 157-170 introduced the luck intuition formally. Sosa, E. (1999). 'How to Defeat Opposition to Moore.' Philosophical Perspectives, 13, 141-153 connects luck to virtue via 'safety': S's belief is safe iff in all nearby possible worlds where S forms the belief in the same way, the belief is true. Zagzebski, L. (1994) showed that any JTB+X analysis can be Gettierised unless it eliminates luck, motivating the virtue-theoretic solution.

entries.label: Epistemic Circularity
entries.hint: Attempts to validate our basic epistemic faculties — perception, memory, inference — are circular: they must use those very faculties to check themselves. We cannot step outside our own cognitive system to verify it from a neutral standpoint. Hume identified the circularity in justifying induction; Descartes' circle is the theological version. The phenomenon sets limits on foundationalist ambitions: even Cartesian foundations require trusting at least the cogito and clear-and-distinct perception before God can be invoked to validate them.
entries.note: Hume, D. (1739). Treatise, Book I, Part III — the circularity of inductive justification. The Cartesian Circle: Arnauld's objection in the Fourth Set of Objections to the Meditations (1641). Alston, W. P. (1986). 'Epistemic Circularity.' Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 47, 1-28 distinguishes vicious from virtuous circularity: track-record arguments for perception's reliability are circular but not viciously so if the circle is wide enough. Sosa, E. (1994). 'Philosophical Scepticism and Epistemic Circularity.' Proceedings of the Aristotelian Society, Supp. 68, 263-290 defends virtuous circularity. The phenomenon motivates externalism (th9, th10).

entries.label: The Lottery Paradox
entries.loc: University of Rochester, Rochester, New York
entries.hint: A fair lottery has one million tickets. For each ticket, you are justified in believing it will lose (the probability is 0.999999). But you also know that exactly one ticket will win. By closure, adding justified beliefs should preserve justification — yet the conjunction of all those justified 'will-lose' beliefs entails that no ticket will win, which you know to be false. The paradox shows that high probability is not sufficient for knowledge or rational full belief, and challenges simple probabilistic accounts of justification.
entries.note: Kyburg, H. E. (1961). Probability and the Logic of Rational Belief. Wesleyan University Press — the paradox is introduced on pp. 197-198. Hawthorne, J. (2004). Knowledge and Lotteries. Oxford University Press — the most thorough treatment: knowledge attributions are sensitive to lottery-style alternatives, motivating contextualism. Williamson, T. (2000). Knowledge and Its Limits connects the lottery to the knowledge norm of assertion: one cannot know a lottery proposition, hence cannot assert it. Repl robust: the paradox is well-formed and no fully satisfying solution is agreed.

entries.label: The Duhem-Quine Underdetermination Thesis
entries.hint: No scientific theory is tested in isolation: a hypothesis H is always tested together with background assumptions and auxiliary hypotheses. When an experiment yields a contrary result, logic alone does not determine which element of the conjunction to reject — the hypothesis, or one of its auxiliaries. Duhem stated this for physics; Quine extended it to all knowledge. The thesis implies that theories are underdetermined by evidence: any body of evidence is compatible with infinitely many theories, each requiring different adjustments to the background.
entries.note: Duhem, P. (1906). La Theorie Physique: Son Objet, Sa Structure. Chevalier et Riviere. Translation: Duhem, P. (1954). The Aim and Structure of Physical Theory. Princeton University Press. Quine, W. V. O. (1951). 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' — the generalisation to all knowledge. Laudan, L. (1990). 'Demystifying Underdetermination.' Minnesota Studies in the Philosophy of Science, 14, 267-297 argues the thesis is trivially true but practically toothless — scientists have heuristics for choosing where to place blame. Stanford, P. K. (2006). Exceeding Our Grasp. Oxford University Press defends a stronger historical version: we have repeatedly failed to consider all serious alternatives.

entries.label: Testimony and the Assurance View
entries.hint: When you believe something because someone told you, how is that belief justified? The reductionist view (Hume) requires independent inductive evidence that testimony is generally reliable. The anti-reductionist view (Reid, Coady) holds that testimony is a basic source of justification — we are entitled to trust others by default. Angell's assurance view (Moran) adds a normative dimension: the speaker gives you their word, taking on a commitment that grounds your belief. How much of our knowledge is 'second-hand' testimony is epistemically basic.
entries.note: Coady, C. A. J. (1992). Testimony: A Philosophical Study. Oxford University Press — anti-reductionism. Moran, R. (2005). 'Getting Told and Being Believed.' Philosophers' Imprint, 5(5) — assurance view. Lackey, J. (2008). Learning from Words: Testimony as a Source of Knowledge. Oxford University Press — reductionism refined. Adler, J. E. (2002). Belief's Own Ethics. MIT Press. The debate connects to social epistemology and Fricker's testimonial injustice: credibility deficits in testimony evaluation can be both epistemically and ethically damaging. Repl mixed: the phenomenon is real; the correct normative account is contested.

entries.label: The Preface Paradox
entries.hint: An author believes each statement in her book — otherwise she would have written differently. But she is also rational in writing in her preface: 'I am sure this book contains errors.' She therefore believes both each individual claim and their conjunction to be false. The paradox shows that rational belief can be inconsistent: the conjunction rule (if you rationally believe P and rationally believe Q, you rationally believe P&Q) fails for large sets of individually justified beliefs, with direct implications for probabilistic theories of rational belief.
entries.note: Makinson, D. C. (1965). 'The Paradox of the Preface.' Analysis, 25(6), 205-207. The paradox is structurally related to the Lottery Paradox: both involve many individually justified beliefs whose conjunction is believed to be false. Christensen, D. (2004). Putting Logic in Its Place. Oxford University Press distinguishes synchronic consistency (required) from logical closure (not required) and uses the preface to argue against the latter. Smithies, D. (2012). 'Moore's Paradox and the Accessibility of Justification.' Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 85, 273-300 connects it to self-knowledge.

entries.label: Echo Chambers and Epistemic Bubbles
entries.loc: University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah
entries.hint: An epistemic bubble is a social network that merely lacks diversity of viewpoints — members could easily encounter outside voices. An echo chamber is more insidious: it actively insulates members from outside voices by undermining the credibility of outsiders. C. Thi Nguyen's distinction matters because the remedies differ: bubbles need only exposure to different information, but echo chambers require rebuilding trust in outsider testimony. Social media recommendation algorithms tend to create both, often simultaneously.
entries.note: Nguyen, C. T. (2020). 'Echo Chambers and Epistemic Bubbles.' Episteme, 17(2), 141-161 — the definitive distinction. Sunstein, C. R. (2017). #Republic: Divided Democracy in the Age of Social Media. Princeton University Press — policy implications. Pariser, E. (2011). The Filter Bubble. Penguin Press — the popular account. Empirical evidence: Bail, C. A. et al. (2018). 'Exposure to Opposing Views on Social Media Can Increase Political Polarization.' PNAS, 115(37), 9216-9221 — surprisingly, exposure to opposing Twitter bots increased polarization, suggesting bubble-bursting may be insufficient. Repl mixed: the conceptual distinction is sound; empirical remedies are contested.

entries.label: The Problem of the Criterion
entries.hint: To identify which of our beliefs constitute knowledge, we need a criterion for knowledge. But to identify the right criterion, we first need to know which beliefs are the known ones. We face a dilemma: begin with particular cases (methodism from particulars — Chisholm's particularism) or begin with the criterion (methodism from principles — the sceptic's approach). Neither avoids circularity or arbitrariness. The problem shows that epistemology cannot bootstrap its way to justified starting points without some prior epistemic commitment.
entries.note: Chisholm, R. M. (1973). The Problem of the Criterion. Marquette University Press — the modern canonical statement. Historical origins in Sextus Empiricus (c. 200 CE). Methodism (starts with criterion) vs. particularism (starts with cases): Chisholm advocates particularism. Sosa, E. (1980) connects the problem to the raft-and-pyramid debate: coherentism is a form of methodism; foundationalism comes closer to particularism. The problem is related to Agrippa's Trilemma: both show the difficulty of finding a non-circular starting point for epistemological justification.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: A Priori / A Posteriori
entries.loc: University of Konigsberg, Konigsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia)
entries.hint: A priori knowledge is justified independently of experience — purely through reason, concept analysis, or rational intuition. A posteriori (empirical) knowledge depends on experience for its justification. Mathematical truths ('2+2=4') and logical tautologies are the paradigm a priori cases; knowledge of the colour of grass is paradigmatically a posteriori. Kant sharpened the distinction by asking whether there is synthetic a priori knowledge — true not by definition yet knowable without experience — arguing there is (space, time, causality as pure intuitions).
entries.note: Kant, I. (1781). Critique of Pure Reason, Introduction — the systematic statement of the distinction. Leibniz, G. W. (1704). New Essays on Human Understanding distinguished 'necessary truths' (a priori) from 'truths of fact' (a posteriori). Quine's 'Two Dogmas' challenged the distinction: if the analytic/synthetic distinction fails, the a priori/a posteriori distinction is also undermined. Kripke, S. A. (1980). Naming and Necessity. Harvard University Press proposed a posteriori necessities (water = H2O) and a priori contingencies, further complicating the picture. Boghossian, P. & Peacocke, C. (Eds.) (2000). New Essays on the A Priori. Oxford University Press is the standard collection.

entries.label: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
entries.loc: University of Konigsberg, Konigsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia)
entries.hint: An analytic statement is true in virtue of the meanings of its terms ('All bachelors are unmarried'). A synthetic statement is true in virtue of how the world is ('The cat is on the mat'). Kant introduced the distinction to carve out space for synthetic a priori knowledge. Logical positivists relied on it to demarcate science (synthetic, empirically verifiable) from metaphysics (neither analytic nor empirically verifiable, hence meaningless). Quine's 'Two Dogmas' (1951) argued that no precise criterion for analyticity can be given without circularity.
entries.note: Kant, I. (1781). Critique of Pure Reason, Introduction A6/B10. Hume anticipated the distinction as 'relations of ideas' vs. 'matters of fact': Enquiry (1748), Section IV. Carnap, R. (1947). Meaning and Necessity. University of Chicago Press — the positivist use. Quine's attack: 'Two Dogmas'. Defence: Grice, H. P. & Strawson, P. F. (1956). 'In Defense of a Dogma.' Philosophical Review, 65, 141-158. Boghossian, P. (1996). 'Analyticity Reconsidered.' Nous, 30, 360-391 revived the distinction by separating epistemological from metaphysical analyticity. The distinction remains contested but is widely used in practice.

entries.label: Propositional vs. Procedural Knowledge (Knowing-That vs. Knowing-How)
entries.loc: Christ Church, University of Oxford, Oxford, England
entries.hint: Gilbert Ryle distinguished knowing-that (propositional knowledge: I know that Paris is the capital of France) from knowing-how (practical knowledge: I know how to ride a bicycle). He argued that the intellectualist legend — the view that all knowing-how reduces to knowing a set of propositions — leads to a regress: applying any rule requires knowing how to apply it, which would require another rule, and so on. Practical competence is irreducible. Wang Yangming's Knowledge-Action Unity offers a complementary insight from the Chinese tradition.
entries.note: Ryle, G. (1949). The Concept of Mind. Hutchinson, ch. 2 — 'Knowing How and Knowing That.' The regress argument against intellectualism: knowing-how cannot be reduced to knowing a proposition about how to proceed without generating an infinite regress of applications. Stanley, J. & Williamson, T. (2001). 'Knowing How.' Journal of Philosophy, 98(8), 411-444 — influential contrary view: knowing-how is a species of knowing-that (propositional knowledge under a practical mode of presentation). Bengson, J. & Moffett, M. A. (Eds.) (2011). Knowing How: Essays on Knowledge, Mind, and Action. Oxford University Press surveys the debate.

entries.label: Wang Yangming's Knowledge-Action Unity (知行合一)
entries.hint: Wang Yangming argued that genuine moral knowledge and moral action are inseparable: if you truly know that something is good, you will act on it; if you fail to act, you have not yet truly known. 'Knowledge' in this context is not propositional information but a living apprehension that manifests in conduct. The view challenges the Western assumption that knowing and doing are independent — that one can fully know a moral truth and freely choose not to act on it. It reframes epistemology as inherently practical and ethical.
entries.note: Wang Yangming (Wang Shouren) (1527). Instructions for Practical Living (Chuan Xi Lu). Translation: Chan, W. T. (1963). Instructions for Practical Living and Other Neo-Confucian Writings by Wang Yang-ming. Columbia University Press. The unity of knowledge and action is a direct challenge to Zhu Xi's Neo-Confucian view that exhaustive investigation of things precedes moral action. Ivanhoe, P. J. (2002). Ethics in the Confucian Tradition. Hackett Publishing examines the epistemological implications. Do not conflate with Ryle's knowing-how: Ryle's distinction is about cognitive categories; Wang's unity is about the inseparability of genuine moral knowledge from its enactment.

entries.label: Epistemic Closure
entries.loc: The Rockefeller University, New York, New York
entries.hint: If you know P, and you know that P entails Q, then you are in a position to know Q. This principle seems obvious but generates sceptical trouble: you know you have hands; you know that having hands entails you are not a brain in a vat; therefore you are in a position to know you are not a brain in a vat. But intuitively you do not know this. Dretske and Nozick denied closure; DeRose's contextualism accepts it while explaining the intuition differently. The principle is central to almost every contemporary debate in epistemology.
entries.note: Dretske, F. (1970). 'Epistemic Operators.' Journal of Philosophy, 67(24), 1007-1023 — denies closure using the relevant alternatives framework: you can know P without being in a position to know every entailment of P that involves irrelevant alternatives. Nozick, R. (1981). Philosophical Explanations. Harvard University Press — tracking theory also denies closure. Hawthorne, J. (2004). Knowledge and Lotteries. Oxford University Press — defends closure and argues denial has unacceptable consequences. DeRose, K. (1995). 'Solving the Sceptical Problem.' Philosophical Review, 104, 1-52 uses closure-preservation within shifting contextual standards to handle the sceptical paradox.

entries.label: Pramana — Valid Means of Knowledge
entries.hint: Classical Indian epistemology organised itself around the question: how many valid means of knowledge (pramana) are there, and what are they? The Carvaka materialists accepted only pratyaksha (perception). Buddhists (Dignaga, Dharmakirti) accepted two: perception and anumana (inference). The Nyaya school accepted four: perception, inference, upamana (comparison/analogy), and shabda (testimony). These debates were as technically rigorous as anything in Western epistemology — they generated logic-like formal systems centuries before European formal logic developed.
entries.note: Nyaya-sutras (Gautama, c. 200 BCE-200 CE) — the foundational Nyaya text enumerating four pramanas. Dignaga (c. 480-540 CE). Pramanasamuccaya (Compendium of Valid Cognition) — the Buddhist reduction to two pramanas and formal epistemological logic. Dharmakirti (c. 600-660 CE). Pramanavarttika (Commentary on Valid Cognition) — the most sophisticated development. Ganeri, J. (2001). Philosophy in Classical India. Routledge surveys the field accessibly. Matilal, B. K. (1986). Perception: An Essay on Classical Indian Theories of Knowledge. Oxford University Press is the standard scholarly reference. The Navya-Nyaya school (Gangesha, 12th century) formalised pramana theory into a precise technical language analogous to modern predicate logic.

entries.label: Fallibilism
entries.hint: We can have knowledge even though our beliefs might be mistaken — knowledge does not require the impossibility of error. Peirce introduced the term; the position is now the default in analytic epistemology, in contrast to Cartesian infallibilism (which requires certainty). Fallibilism permits that our best-justified scientific theories might be false, while still counting as knowledge. Without fallibilism, almost nothing qualifies as knowledge: virtually all our perceptual beliefs are defeasible by evidence we have not yet encountered.
entries.note: Peirce, C. S. (1877). 'The Fixation of Belief.' Popular Science Monthly, 12, 1-15 — the term and the pragmatist framing. Dewey, J. (1929). The Quest for Certainty. Minton, Balch — the classic argument against the Cartesian demand for certainty. Contemporary statement: Dretske, F. (1981). Knowledge and the Flow of Information. MIT Press. The debate over whether fallibilism is compatible with the knowledge norm of assertion (Williamson 2000) is ongoing: if you can know P fallibly, can you assert P without qualifying it? Infallibilism is now a minority position defended mainly by those responding to sceptical arguments by raising the bar for knowledge to certainty.

entries.label: Feminist Epistemology & Standpoint Theory
entries.loc: University of California Los Angeles, California
entries.hint: Standpoint theory holds that one's social position — particularly membership in a marginalised group — generates a distinctive epistemic advantage. The standpoint theorist's key argument is that the oppressed must understand both their own situation and the dominant culture's norms in order to navigate and survive; the dominant group has no reciprocal need to understand the marginalised perspective. This 'dual consciousness' (drawing on W.E.B. Du Bois) produces a more complete, less distorted account of social reality. Sandra Harding distinguishes weak standpoint theory (marginalised perspectives are valuable and should be included) from strong standpoint theory (they are epistemically superior in specific domains). Donna Haraway's 'situated knowledges' reformulates this without the superiority claim: all knowledge is produced from a situated perspective, and acknowledging that situatedness is the condition of genuine objectivity — as opposed to the 'god-trick' of claiming a view from nowhere.
entries.note: Hartsock, N. (1983). 'The Feminist Standpoint: Developing the Ground for a Specifically Feminist Historical Materialism.' In Harding, S. & Hintikka, M. (Eds.), Discovering Reality. Reidel, 283-310 — the founding paper. Harding, S. (1986). The Science Question in Feminism. Cornell University Press. Harding, S. (1991). Whose Science? Whose Knowledge? Cornell University Press — the systematic treatment, introducing 'strong objectivity' and distinguishing weak from strong standpoint claims. Haraway, D. (1988). 'Situated Knowledges: The Science Question in Feminism and the Privilege of Partial Perspective.' Feminist Studies, 14(3), 575-599 — situated knowledges and the critique of the god-trick. Du Bois, W. E. B. (1903). The Souls of Black Folk. A.C. McClurg & Co. — double-consciousness as the proto-standpoint concept. Collins, P. H. (1990). Black Feminist Thought. Routledge — matrix of domination and the centrality of lived experience in epistemology. Critical responses: Longino, H. (1990). Science as Social Knowledge. Princeton University Press — contextual values and the social nature of scientific enquiry as an alternative to standpoint theory.

entries.label: Ubuntu & Indigenous Relational Epistemologies
entries.hint: Ubuntu — 'I am because we are' (Nguni: umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu) — offers a fundamentally relational epistemology: knowledge is not possessed by an isolated individual knower but constituted through communal relationship, dialogue, and participation. This contrasts sharply with the Cartesian tradition's solitary thinker and parallels Indigenous North and South American frameworks in which knowledge is inseparable from specific lands, relationships, and oral traditions. Kwame Gyekye argues that Ubuntu communal epistemology locates epistemic competence in community without denying individual contribution. Vine Deloria Jr.'s critique of Western academic knowledge argues that Indigenous knowledge systems — grounded in ceremony, ecological observation, and inter-generational oral transmission — constitute valid epistemic alternatives that cannot be reduced to the justified-true-belief analysis. The common thread across these traditions is that knowledge is an active ethical relationship rather than a passive representation of an external world.
entries.note: Wiredu, K. (1980). Philosophy and an African Culture. Cambridge University Press — the first systematic anglophone African philosophy, situating Ubuntu-type communalism within analytic terms. Tutu, D. (1999). No Future Without Forgiveness. Rider — Ubuntu as ethical and epistemological framework in post-apartheid reconciliation. Deloria, V. Jr. (1969). Custer Died for Your Sins. Macmillan. Deloria, V. Jr. & Wildcat, D. R. (2001). Power and Place: Indian Education in America. Fulcrum Publishing — Indigenous knowledge as relational and land-based. Smith, L. T. (1999). Decolonizing Methodologies: Research and Indigenous Peoples. Zed Books — the foundational text on Indigenous research epistemology, widely used in qualitative methodology training. Gyekye, K. (1997). Tradition and Modernity: Philosophical Reflections on the African Experience. Oxford University Press — defends the compatibility of Ubuntu communal epistemology with individual epistemic rights. Hountondji, P. J. (1983). African Philosophy: Myth and Reality. Indiana University Press — cautions against treating African philosophy as a homogeneous tradition.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Thought Experiment
entries.hint: Philosophy's primary laboratory instrument: a carefully constructed hypothetical scenario designed to isolate and test our intuitions about a concept. Gettier counterexamples, Putnam's twin earth, the trolley problem, the original position — all are thought experiments. Their epistemic status is contested: do they track conceptual truths, moral facts, or merely culturally contingent intuitions? Experimental philosophy emerged partly to test whether philosophical thought experiments generalise across cultures.
entries.note: Methodological discussion: Brown, J. R. (1991). The Laboratory of the Mind: Thought Experiments in the Natural Sciences. Routledge. Sorensen, R. A. (1992). Thought Experiments. Oxford University Press. Norton, J. D. (1996). 'Are Thought Experiments Just What You Thought?' Canadian Journal of Philosophy, 26, 333-366 — reconstructs thought experiments as ordinary arguments in disguise. Challenge from experimental philosophy: Weinberg, J. M., Nichols, S. & Stich, S. (2001). 'Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions.' Philosophical Topics, 29, 429-460 showed that responses to Gettier-style cases vary across cultures, questioning their universality as evidence.

entries.label: Reflective Equilibrium
entries.hint: The method of justifying principles by achieving coherence between general principles and particular considered judgements — adjusting each in light of the other until they mutually support one another. Goodman introduced it in the philosophy of induction; Rawls extended it to moral philosophy (the original position is justified partly by its coherence with our considered moral judgements). Applied to epistemology: we justify epistemic principles by testing them against cases in which we are confident something is or is not knowledge.
entries.note: Goodman, N. (1955). Fact, Fiction, and Forecast. Harvard University Press, ch. 3 — the original formulation in inductive logic. Rawls, J. (1971). A Theory of Justice. Harvard University Press, Section 9 — the moral philosophy application. DePaul, M. R. (1993). Balance and Refinement: Beyond Coherence Methods of Moral Inquiry. Routledge examines the method's limits. Criticism: Stich, S. P. (1988). 'Reflective Equilibrium, Analytic Epistemology, and the Problem of Cognitive Diversity.' Synthese, 74, 391-413 — if intuitions vary by culture, reflective equilibrium may just systematise parochial assumptions. Connects to coherentism in its rejection of a fixed foundational starting point.

entries.label: Conceptual Analysis
entries.loc: Trinity College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, England
entries.hint: The method of specifying the necessary and sufficient conditions for the application of a concept by testing candidate analyses against intuitive cases. 'Knowledge = justified true belief' is a paradigm product. Moore's method of analysis — breaking complex concepts into their simple constituents — set the agenda for 20th-century analytic philosophy. The Gettier problem is the paradigm failure of conceptual analysis: the proposed conditions for 'knowledge' were shown to be insufficient. Whether analysis is possible at all is now itself a contested methodological question.
entries.note: Moore, G. E. (1903). Principia Ethica. Cambridge University Press — the method applied to 'good.' Russell, B. (1905). 'On Denoting.' Mind, 14, 479-493 — analysis of definite descriptions. Criticism: Wittgenstein, L. (1953). Philosophical Investigations — meaning as use undermines the project of necessary-and-sufficient-conditions analysis. Fodor, J. A. (1998). Concepts: Where Cognitive Science Went Wrong. Oxford University Press — concepts do not have definitional structure. Jackson, F. (1998). From Metaphysics to Ethics. Oxford University Press defends conceptual analysis via the method of cases. The practice continues under the label 'analytic epistemology' despite sustained methodological scepticism.

entries.label: Bayesian Epistemology
entries.hint: Rational belief comes in degrees (credences) that obey the probability calculus; rational updating on new evidence follows Bayes' theorem — posterior credence = (prior x likelihood) / evidence probability. Bayesianism makes precise ideas that are otherwise vague: what it means to confirm a hypothesis, how strong evidence changes belief, and when prior beliefs are rational. It is the dominant formal framework in philosophy of science, statistics, and decision theory, though the problem of the prior (how to set initial credences) remains unsolved.
entries.note: Savage, L. J. (1954). The Foundations of Statistics. Wiley — subjective Bayesianism. Carnap, R. (1950). Logical Foundations of Probability. University of Chicago Press — logical (objective) Bayesianism. Howson, C. & Urbach, P. (1989). Scientific Reasoning: The Bayesian Approach. Open Court — the standard epistemological defence. Problem of the prior: how should initial credences be set before any evidence? Objective Bayesians (Jaynes) use maximum entropy; subjective Bayesians (de Finetti) allow any coherent prior. The problem of old evidence: Glymour, C. (1980). Theory and Evidence. Princeton University Press — Bayesian confirmation has trouble explaining how known evidence can confirm a new theory.

entries.label: Experimental Philosophy (X-Phi)
entries.loc: Rutgers University, New Brunswick, New Jersey
entries.hint: Experimental philosophers use empirical methods — surveys, reaction-time studies, cross-cultural questionnaires — to test whether the intuitions philosophers rely on are universal or culturally contingent. In epistemology, X-Phi has probed whether Gettier judgements are consistent across cultures, whether the stakes-sensitivity of knowledge attributions is real, and whether folk concepts of knowledge match the theorist's assumptions. The field challenges the authority of armchair philosophical intuition as evidence.
entries.note: Weinberg, J. M., Nichols, S. & Stich, S. (2001). 'Normativity and Epistemic Intuitions.' Philosophical Topics, 29, 429-460 — the founding X-Phi paper in epistemology: Gettier intuitions varied across East Asian and South Asian subjects vs. Western subjects. Knobe, J. & Nichols, S. (Eds.) (2008). Experimental Philosophy. Oxford University Press. Criticism: Ludwig, K. (2007). 'The Epistemology of Thought Experiments: First Person versus Third Person Approaches.' Midwest Studies in Philosophy, 31, 128-159 argues X-Phi tests folk concepts, not the theorist's refined concepts. Replication: Kim, M. & Yuan, Y. (2015). 'No Cross-Cultural Differences in the Gettier Car Case Intuition.' Episteme, 12, 355-361 — did not replicate cross-cultural variation for that case.

entries.label: Phenomenological Method
entries.hint: Husserl's phenomenological method brackets the natural attitude — the commonsense assumption that the external world exists — and examines the structures of conscious experience as they present themselves. Rather than asking whether the external world exists (the sceptical question), phenomenology asks how objects, other minds, time, and selfhood are constituted in experience. Phenomenology makes first-person, introspective description rigorous by exposing the invariant structures (intentionality, temporal flow, the horizon) that all experience shares.
entries.note: Husserl, E. (1900). Logical Investigations. Max Niemeyer Verlag — the founding work. Husserl, E. (1913). Ideas I (Ideen zu einer reinen Phanomenologie). Max Niemeyer — introduces the epoché (bracketing). Heidegger, M. (1927). Being and Time. Max Niemeyer — transforms phenomenology into an existential analytic. Merleau-Ponty, M. (1945). Phenomenology of Perception. Gallimard — embodied phenomenology challenges Cartesian dualism and the primacy of propositional knowledge. Smith, D. W. (2007). Husserl. Routledge. The method's relation to mainstream analytic epistemology is contested: phenomenologists often regard analytic epistemology's reliance on thought experiments as missing the first-person, experiential dimension of knowledge.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Plato
entries.hint: Plato's Theaetetus (~369 BCE) is the first systematic investigation of knowledge. Socrates methodically demolishes three proposed definitions — knowledge as perception (refuted by argument from perceptual relativity), as true belief (refuted by the possibility of true belief without understanding), and as true belief with a logos or account (shown to face a regress). The dialogue ends without a positive definition: the aporetic structure itself is the lesson. His theory of recollection (Meno) proposed that all genuine knowledge is a priori — souls remember eternal Forms, making learning a form of remembering.
entries.note: Plato. Theaetetus (~369 BCE). Meno (~385 BCE). Standard edition: Cooper, J. M. (Ed.) (1997). Plato: Complete Works. Hackett. Cornford, F. M. (1935). Plato's Theory of Knowledge. Kegan Paul — the standard commentary on the Theaetetus. The third definition in the Theaetetus (true belief + logos) is the ancestor of the JTB analysis; Gettier's (1963) three-page demolition mirrors the dialogue's structure. Plato's Forms as objects of a priori knowledge directly anticipate debates about rationalism (Group 3) and the synthetic a priori (fg4, Kant).

entries.label: René Descartes
entries.hint: Descartes founded modern epistemology by replacing the Aristotelian reliance on the senses with a first-person rational inquiry. His method of doubt and the cogito — 'I think, therefore I am' — established the template for three centuries of epistemology: begin from radical sceptical doubt, locate one incorrigible certainty, rebuild knowledge on it. The Cartesian picture — an individual rational thinker verifying beliefs from an internal standpoint — is the explicit or implicit target of almost every subsequent development in the field, from Hume's scepticism to social and feminist epistemology.
entries.note: Descartes, R. (1641). Meditationes de Prima Philosophia. Michel Soly. Descartes, R. (1637). Discours de la Methode. Cottingham, J. (1986). Descartes. Blackwell — the standard philosophical biography. The Cartesian Circle (Arnauld's objection): the cogito seems to rely on God's veracity to validate clear-and-distinct perception, but God's existence is proved using clear-and-distinct perception. Wilson, M. D. (1978). Descartes. Routledge is the standard scholarly treatment. Descartes' influence on foundationalism is direct; his influence in motivating every subsequent rejection of first-person, rationalist, foundationalist epistemology is equally pervasive.

entries.label: David Hume
entries.hint: Hume set the two central problems of analytic epistemology: the problem of induction (we cannot rationally justify inferring future from past) and the problem of causation (we perceive constant conjunction but never necessary connection). His distinction between 'relations of ideas' (what Kant called a priori) and 'matters of fact' (a posteriori) underpins the analytic/synthetic distinction. Kant credited Hume with waking him from his 'dogmatic slumber.' Hume's naturalistic resolution — induction is a habit of mind, not a rational inference — anticipates Quine's naturalised epistemology.
entries.note: Hume, D. (1739). A Treatise of Human Nature. John Noon. Hume, D. (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding. A. Millar. Stroud, B. (1977). Hume. Routledge — the standard secondary text. Hume, D. (1779). Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion. Beaumont Blackburn — posthumously published, applies the same sceptical toolkit to theological knowledge. Al-Ghazali's parallel scepticism about causation predates Hume by six centuries (st10, fg11), though there is no evidence of textual transmission. Hume's influence runs through Kant, Quine, and every contemporary debate about the a priori.

entries.label: Immanuel Kant
entries.loc: University of Konigsberg, Konigsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia)
entries.hint: Kant's Critique of Pure Reason (1781) is the most ambitious epistemological work in the Western tradition. He proposed that the mind actively structures experience: space, time, and causality are not features of the world as it is in itself but forms of intuition and understanding the mind imposes on sensory input. This 'Copernican revolution' yielded synthetic a priori knowledge — necessary truths about space and causality that are not merely definitional. Non-Euclidean geometry later challenged his specific claim about space, but the Kantian framework of active, structuring cognition shaped every subsequent debate about the a priori and the limits of knowledge.
entries.note: Kant, I. (1781). Kritik der reinen Vernunft. Johann Friedrich Hartknoch. Standard translation: Kemp Smith, N. (1929). Critique of Pure Reason. Macmillan. Allison, H. (2004). Kant's Transcendental Idealism (revised edition). Yale University Press — the standard contemporary defence. Non-Euclidean geometry (Bolyai, Lobachevsky, 1820s-1830s) and general relativity undermined the claim that space is necessarily Euclidean. Strawson, P. F. (1966). The Bounds of Sense. Methuen — influential reconstruction stripping transcendental idealism from Kant's epistemology. The synthetic a priori debate is directly Kantian in origin.

entries.label: W.V.O. Quine
entries.hint: Quine dismantled the two central tenets of logical empiricism and replaced the traditional epistemological project. 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' (1951) dissolved the analytic/synthetic distinction and proposed that the whole web of belief — including logic and mathematics — faces the tribunal of experience together. 'Epistemology Naturalized' (1969) proposed that epistemology should become a chapter of cognitive psychology. Together these papers ended logical positivism as a programme, made naturalism the default methodology, and reshaped the agenda of anglophone philosophy for half a century.
entries.note: Quine, W. V. O. (1953). From a Logical Point of View. Harvard University Press — collects Two Dogmas and related papers. Quine, W. V. O. (1969). Ontological Relativity and Other Essays. Columbia University Press — includes Epistemology Naturalized. Orenstein, A. (2002). W.V. Quine. Princeton University Press is the biographical and philosophical overview. Quine's challenge to the analytic/synthetic distinction is contested but foundationally influential; his naturalised epistemology programme generated a sustained literature on whether normativity can survive naturalism.

entries.label: Edmund Gettier
entries.hint: Gettier published one paper — three pages in the journal Analysis — and transformed epistemology. His counterexamples showed that the tripartite JTB analysis, which had organised the field for two millennia, is insufficient for knowledge. The paper triggered more than fifty years of analysis without consensus on a fourth condition. Gettier himself published virtually nothing else in philosophy; his name became both a label for an entire class of counterexamples and the designation for the central preoccupation of post-1963 analytic epistemology.
entries.note: Gettier, E. L. (1963). 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' Analysis, 23(6), 121-123. The paper had reportedly been rejected by Mind before Analysis accepted it. Gettier published no further research articles in epistemology. Shope, R. K. (1983). The Analysis of Knowing. Princeton University Press catalogues over a hundred proposed fourth conditions. Zagzebski, L. (1994). 'The Inescapability of Gettier Problems.' Philosophical Quarterly, 44, 65-73 proves that any JTB+X analysis is vulnerable to Gettier-style construction as long as justification and truth are independent — suggesting the analysis project is structurally flawed.

entries.label: Alvin Goldman
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: Goldman founded process reliabilism in 1979 and built it into a comprehensive epistemological system in Epistemology and Cognition (1986), making him the defining externalist of the late twentieth century. He subsequently founded social epistemology as a systematic programme — applying reliabilist thinking to the evaluation of epistemic institutions (courts, science, education, the media) by their truth-producing reliability. His work established the decisive bridge between traditional analytic epistemology and cognitive science.
entries.note: Goldman, A. I. (1979). 'What Is Justified Belief?' In Pappas, G. (Ed.), Justification and Knowledge. Reidel, 1-23. Goldman, A. I. (1986). Epistemology and Cognition. Harvard University Press. Goldman, A. I. (1999). Knowledge in a Social World. Oxford University Press — the social epistemology programme. Goldman, A. I. (2012). Reliabilism and Contemporary Epistemology. Oxford University Press. The generality problem for reliabilism (Conee & Feldman 1998) is the main persistent objection; Goldman's responses have progressively tightened the specification of relevant process types.

entries.label: Ernest Sosa
entries.hint: Sosa founded virtue reliabilism and developed the raft-and-pyramid framework (1980) that reframed the foundationalism vs. coherentism debate. His central distinction is between animal knowledge (a true belief that manifests a reliable cognitive faculty) and reflective knowledge (the believer can also give a satisfactory account of why the faculty is reliable). This two-tier account preserves both the externalist insight (reliability matters) and the internalist intuition (reflective understanding matters), and directly addresses BonJour's clairvoyant objection.
entries.note: Sosa, E. (1980). 'The Raft and the Pyramid.' Midwest Studies in Philosophy, 5, 3-25. Sosa, E. (1991). Knowledge in Perspective. Cambridge University Press. Sosa, E. (2007). A Virtue Epistemology: Apt Belief and Reflective Knowledge, vol. 1. Oxford University Press. Sosa, E. (2015). Judgment and Agency. Oxford University Press. Received the Nicholas Rescher Prize (2010) and Jean Nicod Prize (2011). The animal/reflective knowledge distinction is the most influential architectural feature of contemporary virtue epistemology; Greco (2010) and Zagzebski (1996) develop it in different directions.

entries.label: Miranda Fricker
entries.loc: Birkbeck, University of London, London, England
entries.hint: Fricker's Epistemic Injustice (2007) opened a subdiscipline at the intersection of epistemology and political philosophy. By identifying testimonial injustice and hermeneutical injustice as wrongs done to a person specifically in their capacity as a knower, she showed that epistemic norms are not socially neutral. The same credibility-assessment processes that generate knowledge can also produce injustice when contaminated by identity prejudice. Her work catalysed a generation of scholarship connecting epistemology to race, gender, disability, and colonial power structures.
entries.note: Fricker, M. (2007). Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethical Life of Knowledge. Oxford University Press. BSPS Annual Book Prize 2007. Fricker, M. (2017). 'Evolving Concepts of Epistemic Injustice.' In Kidd, Medina & Pohlhaus (Eds.), The Routledge Handbook of Epistemic Injustice. Routledge. Extensions: Dotson, K. (2011). 'Tracking Epistemic Violence.' Hypatia, 26(2), 236-257. Mason, E. (2011). 'Moral Ignorance and Blameworthiness.' Philosophical Studies, 152(2). Pohlhaus, G. (2012). 'Relational Knowing and Epistemic Injustice.' Hypatia, 27(4). Fricker's subsequent work extends to broader questions of epistemic agency and institutional responsibility.

entries.label: Timothy Williamson
entries.hint: Williamson's Knowledge and Its Limits (2000) inverted the agenda of analytic epistemology: rather than analysing knowledge in terms of belief, justification, and truth, he argued that knowledge is irreducible and should be the starting point. His E=K thesis (evidence = knowledge), the knowledge norm of assertion, and the treatment of margins of error in vague contexts established him as the most influential epistemologist of the early twenty-first century. His formal methods — deploying modal logic, possible-world semantics, and decision theory — also bridged epistemology and philosophy of language.
entries.note: Williamson, T. (2000). Knowledge and Its Limits. Oxford University Press. Williamson, T. (2007). The Philosophy of Philosophy. Blackwell — defends armchair methodology against X-Phi. Williamson, T. (2013). Modal Logic as Metaphysics. Oxford University Press. Critical literature: Nagel, J. (2014). Knowledge: A Very Short Introduction. Oxford University Press surveys the landscape shaped by Williamson. Conee, E. & Feldman, R. (2004). Evidentialism — direct opposition to E=K. The knowledge-first programme has generated responses in every subdomain of epistemology, from perception and memory to legal and moral epistemology.

entries.label: Al-Ghazali
entries.hint: Al-Ghazali is the most important epistemologist in the Islamic philosophical tradition. His Deliverance from Error (c. 1095) employs systematic methodological doubt five centuries before Descartes, and his critique of Aristotelian causality — arguing that necessary causal connection is unobservable and only God creates constant conjunctions — anticipates Hume by six centuries. He integrated epistemology with theological and mystical inquiry, producing a model of knowledge that differs fundamentally from the secular Cartesian tradition: his resolution is through divine illumination, not the cogito.
entries.note: Al-Ghazali (c. 1095). Al-Munqidh min al-Dalal (Deliverance from Error). Al-Ghazali (1095). Tahafut al-Falasifa (Incoherence of the Philosophers). Translation of Deliverance: McCarthy, R. J. (1980). Freedom and Fulfillment. Twayne Publishers. Griffel, F. (2009). Al-Ghazali's Philosophical Theology. Oxford University Press — the definitive scholarly study. Frank, R. M. (1992). Creation and the Cosmic System: Al-Ghazali and Avicenna. Heidelberg. Leaman, O. (2002) documents the structural parallels with Descartes and Hume but notes that al-Ghazali's resolution through divine light (nur) has no secular analogue — the parallel is structural, not philosophical.

entries.label: Nagarjuna
entries.hint: Nagarjuna's Mulamadhyamakakarika (Fundamental Verses on the Middle Way, c. 150-250 CE) uses prasanga (reductio ad absurdum) to argue that all phenomena lack inherent existence (svabhava): nothing exists independently, permanently, or from its own side. His two-truths doctrine distinguishes conventional truth (the level at which tables and persons exist) from ultimate truth (at which inherent existence is absent). The epistemological implication is profound: our standard concepts presuppose inherent existence and thus systematically misrepresent ultimate reality, making ordinary propositional knowledge intrinsically conventional and limited.
entries.note: Nagarjuna (c. 150-250 CE). Mulamadhyamakakarika. Translation and commentary: Garfield, J. L. (1995). The Fundamental Wisdom of the Middle Way. Oxford University Press. Siderits, M. & Katsura, S. (2013). Nagarjuna's Middle Way. Wisdom Publications. Priest, G. & Garfield, J. (2002). 'Nagarjuna and the Limits of Thought.' Philosophy East and West, 52(1), 1-21 — formal analysis of the two-truths doctrine. Caution: Nagarjuna's scepticism is not Cartesian — it does not doubt perception in conventional terms but challenges the metaphysical presupposition of inherent existence that underlies Western epistemology's concept of objective knowledge. The Gangesha (12th century) Navya-Nyaya school (see co7) developed the competing realist tradition.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: The Post-Gettier Analysis Problem
entries.hint: Fifty years of proposals for a fourth condition to supplement JTB have not produced consensus.
