Four families have been explored: (1) no-false-lemma conditions — the inference to the true belief must not pass through a false step; (2) defeasibility conditions — the justification must survive the addition of all truths; (3) sensitivity conditions — the belief must track the truth counterfactually; (4) safety and virtue conditions — the belief must arise from a competence that could not easily produce a false belief. Zagzebski (1994) argues the project is structurally doomed: any JTB+X analysis can be Gettierised as long as justification and truth are independent.
entries.note: Clark, M. (1963). 'Knowledge and Grounds.' Analysis, 24, 46-48 — the no-false-lemma response. Lehrer, K. & Paxson, T. (1969). 'Knowledge: Undefeated Justified True Belief.' Journal of Philosophy, 66, 225-237. Nozick, R. (1981). Philosophical Explanations. Harvard University Press, ch. 3 — the sensitivity (tracking) condition. Pritchard, D. (2005). Epistemic Luck. Oxford University Press — the safety condition. Greco, J. (2010). Achieving Knowledge. Cambridge University Press — virtue-theoretic resolution. Zagzebski, L. (1994). 'The Inescapability of Gettier Problems.' Philosophical Quarterly, 44, 65-73 — the structural impossibility result. Williamson draws the lesson that analysis itself is the wrong project.

entries.label: The Analytic-Synthetic Debate
entries.hint: Quine's 'Two Dogmas' (1951) argued that no precise, non-circular criterion for analyticity can be given — the category of 'true by meaning alone' cannot be cleanly separated from 'true and also very well confirmed.' This challenge goes to the heart of logical empiricism and of any epistemology that relies on a priori conceptual analysis. The debate has three live positions: Quinean holism (no analytic/synthetic distinction); moderate analyticity (some conceptual truths are epistemologically prior, even if the boundary is fuzzy); and neo-Kantian accounts that locate analyticity in the normative structure of inquiry rather than in meanings.
entries.note: Quine, W. V. O. (1951). 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism.' Philosophical Review, 60(1), 20-43. Defence: Grice, H. P. & Strawson, P. F. (1956). 'In Defense of a Dogma.' Philosophical Review, 65, 141-158. Boghossian, P. (1996). 'Analyticity Reconsidered.' Nous, 30, 360-391 — revives epistemological analyticity. Williamson, T. (2007). The Philosophy of Philosophy, ch. 3 argues that even if analyticity exists, it cannot play the epistemological role assigned to it. Juhl, C. & Loomis, E. (2010). Analyticity. Routledge is the standard textbook treatment of the contemporary debate.

entries.label: Internalism vs. Externalism
entries.hint: The deepest fault line in contemporary epistemology. Internalism: justification depends only on factors the believer can access by reflection — reasons, evidence in the mind. Externalism: justification can depend on external factors such as process reliability or environmental safety, inaccessible to the believer. Internalism preserves the intuition that two believers with identical internal states are equally justified; externalism allows that the brain in a vat is less justified than its real-world twin despite identical internal states. The debate ramifies across every subdomain: moral epistemology, social epistemology, and the epistemology of perception all turn on it.
entries.note: The debate crystallised with Goldman (1979) as the externalist provocation and BonJour's clairvoyant as the internalist response. Chisholm, R. M. (1966). Theory of Knowledge — paradigm internalism. Alston, W. P. (1988). 'An Internalist Externalism.' Synthese, 74, 265-283 attempts synthesis. Fumerton, R. (1995). Metaepistemology and Skepticism — systematic internalism. Sosa, E. (1991) proposes the animal/reflective distinction as a way of accommodating both intuitions. Pritchard, D. (2012). Epistemological Disjunctivism. Oxford University Press develops a novel position on the boundary.

entries.label: Peer Disagreement — Conciliationism vs. Steadfastness
entries.hint: When an epistemic peer — someone with the same evidence and reasoning ability — disagrees with you, what should you do? Conciliationists (Elga, Christensen) say you must move toward your peer's view: treating yourself as more reliable is arbitrary. Steadfasters (Kelly) say you may maintain your view if your own assessment of the evidence is correct: the mere fact of disagreement is not itself evidence that you erred. The debate has practical stakes: expert disagreement on science policy, medical treatment, and ethical matters shapes public belief and institutional response.
entries.note: Kelly, T. (2005). 'The Epistemic Significance of Disagreement.' Oxford Studies in Epistemology, 1, 167-196 — steadfastness. Elga, A. (2007). 'Reflection and Disagreement.' Nous, 41(3), 478-502 — conciliationism. Christensen, D. (2007). 'Epistemology of Disagreement: The Good News.' Philosophical Review, 116(2), 187-217. Lackey, J. (2010). 'A Justificationist View of Disagreement's Epistemic Significance.' In Haddock, Millar & Pritchard (Eds.), Social Epistemology. Oxford University Press — intermediate position. The debate connects to peer disagreement in democratic theory: Estlund, D. (2008). Democratic Authority. Princeton University Press.

entries.label: Scientific Realism vs. Anti-Realism
entries.hint: Scientific realism holds that successful scientific theories are approximately true and that theoretical entities (electrons, quarks) exist independently of our theories. Anti-realism denies one or both claims: van Fraassen's constructive empiricism accepts only that theories are empirically adequate (save the phenomena); Kuhn's incommensurability suggests that 'truth' cannot be the goal of successive paradigms because rivals cannot share a common standard. The debate turns on the argument from no miracles (realism: what else explains science's predictive success?) versus the argument from pessimistic meta-induction (anti-realism: past successful theories were abandoned, so we should expect current ones to be too).
entries.note: van Fraassen, B. C. (1980). The Scientific Image. Oxford University Press — constructive empiricism. Putnam, H. (1975). Mathematics, Matter and Method. Cambridge University Press — the no-miracles argument. Laudan, L. (1981). 'A Confutation of Convergent Realism.' Philosophy of Science, 48, 19-49 — pessimistic meta-induction. Psillos, S. (1999). Scientific Realism: How Science Tracks Truth. Routledge is the standard realist response. Chakravartty, A. (2017). Scientific Ontology. Oxford University Press — a contemporary realist synthesis. Kuhn's incommensurability thesis is a resource for anti-realism but Kuhn resisted the fully anti-realist reading.

entries.label: The Extended Mind Thesis
entries.loc: Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, Missouri
entries.hint: Clark and Chalmers (1998) argued that the mind and its knowledge are not bounded by brain and skin: if a cognitive process in the world functions the same way as a neural process inside the skull, it is part of the mind. Otto's notebook (which he consults for addresses as others consult memory) is part of Otto's mind; the entries in it count as Otto's beliefs. The thesis challenges the Cartesian assumption that knowledge is an internal, first-person state and raises questions about cognitive offloading, digital memory, and the epistemology of extended cognitive systems.
entries.note: Clark, A. & Chalmers, D. (1998). 'The Extended Mind.' Analysis, 58(1), 7-19 — the founding paper. Clark, A. (2008). Supersizing the Mind. Oxford University Press — the book-length defence. Objections: Adams, F. & Aizawa, K. (2001). 'The Bounds of Cognition.' Philosophical Psychology, 14, 43-64 — the coupling-constitution fallacy: being functionally coupled to a cognitive system does not make it part of your mind. Rupert, R. D. (2004). 'Challenges to the Hypothesis of Extended Cognition.' Journal of Philosophy, 101, 389-428. The epistemological implications: Pritchard, D. (2010). 'Cognitive Ability and the Extended Cognition Thesis.' Synthese, 175, 133-151 — can knowledge be extended in the same sense as mind?

entries.label: Epistemic Relativism
entries.hint: Epistemic relativism holds that what counts as knowledge, justification, or rationality varies across cultures, communities, or conceptual schemes — there is no view from nowhere. The Edinburgh Strong Programme in the sociology of scientific knowledge (Bloor, Barnes) argued that the social causes of belief are symmetric: success and failure, truth and falsity, should be explained by the same sociological factors. Feminist standpoint theory holds that marginalised positions yield epistemic advantages. Opposed to both is the insight that relativism seems self-refuting: the claim that all knowledge is relative appears itself to make an absolute claim.
entries.note: Barnes, B. & Bloor, D. (1982). 'Relativism, Rationalism and the Sociology of Knowledge.' In Hollis, M. & Lukes, S. (Eds.), Rationality and Relativism. MIT Press. Bloor, D. (1976). Knowledge and Social Imagery. Routledge — the Strong Programme. Boghossian, P. (2006). Fear of Knowledge: Against Relativism and Constructivism. Oxford University Press — the most sustained contemporary anti-relativist argument. Harding, S. (1991). Whose Science? Whose Knowledge? Cornell University Press — standpoint theory as an alternative to strong relativism. The self-refutation problem: any argument for relativism must either be relatively good (relative to some standard) or absolutely good (contradicting the thesis).

entries.label: Synthetic A Priori Knowledge — Is There Any?
entries.loc: University of Konigsberg, Konigsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad, Russia)
entries.hint: Kant claimed that arithmetic, geometry, and the causal principle are synthetic a priori — necessarily true yet not derivable from definitions alone. Logical positivists denied this: all a priori truths are analytic (Carnap). Quine dissolved the distinction entirely. But Kripke (1980) introduced a new complexity: some necessary truths are a posteriori (water is H2O), and some contingent truths may be a priori. This restructuring is now contested: what exactly distinguishes the a priori from the empirical, and does mathematics give us genuine synthetic a priori knowledge, or is it analytic, or empirical in a disguised way?
entries.note: Kant, I. (1781). Critique of Pure Reason, Introduction. Ayer, A. J. (1936). Language, Truth and Logic. Gollancz — logical positivist denial: all a priori truths are analytic. Kripke, S. A. (1980). Naming and Necessity. Harvard University Press — necessary a posteriori and contingent a priori cases. BonJour, L. (1998). In Defense of Pure Reason. Cambridge University Press — defends synthetic a priori against Quine. Jenkins, C. S. I. (2008). Grounding Concepts. Oxford University Press — empiricist account of a priori knowledge via concept grounding. Field, H. (2005). 'Recent Debates about the A Priori.' Oxford Studies in Epistemology, 1, 69-88 surveys the contemporary landscape.

entries.label: Is Naturalised Epistemology Self-Undermining?
entries.hint: Quine proposed that epistemology become a chapter of cognitive psychology. Kim's objection: this is self-undermining. If we use the methods of natural science to validate our natural science-forming processes, we argue in a circle. Worse: traditional epistemology was normative — it told us how we should form beliefs, not just how we do. A purely descriptive cognitive science cannot generate the normative conclusions epistemology needs. Goldman's reliabilism attempts a solution: normativity survives in the distinction between reliable and unreliable belief-forming processes, where reliability is naturalistically defined.
entries.note: Kim, J. (1988). 'What Is Naturalized Epistemology?' Philosophical Perspectives, 2, 381-405 — the canonical objection. Quine, W. V. O. (1969). 'Epistemology Naturalized' — the programme. Kornblith, H. (1994). (Ed.) Naturalizing Epistemology. MIT Press — a collection of naturalist responses. Stroud, B. (1996). 'Epistemological Reflection on Knowledge of the External World.' Philosophy and Phenomenological Research, 56, 345-358 — the irreducibility of the normative. Steup, M. & Sosa, E. (Eds.) (2005). Contemporary Debates in Epistemology. Blackwell, Part VII — the best single collection representing both sides.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Legal Epistemology — Evidence and Testimony in Law
entries.hint: Courts are epistemic institutions: they must determine facts from testimony, physical evidence, and expert witnesses using formal rules about admissibility and reliability. Legal epistemology examines the standards of proof (beyond reasonable doubt, balance of probabilities), the reliability of eyewitness testimony, the epistemic authority of expert witnesses, and whether legal verdicts can be said to constitute knowledge. Fricker's testimonial injustice has direct implications: racial and gender bias in credibility judgements contaminate legal fact-finding.
entries.note: Wigmore, J. H. (1913). The Principles of Judicial Proof. Little, Brown — the foundational systematic treatment. Loftus, E. F. & Palmer, J. C. (1974). 'Reconstruction of Automobile Destruction.' Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior, 13, 585-589 — eyewitness testimony is reconstructive and subject to post-event information. Goldman, A. I. (1999). Knowledge in a Social World. Oxford University Press, ch. 9 — legal epistemology from a reliabilist perspective. Allen, R. J. & Leiter, B. (2001). 'Naturalized Epistemology and the Law of Evidence.' Virginia Law Review, 87, 1491-1550. Haack, S. (2014). Evidence Matters: Science, Proof, and Truth in the Law. Cambridge University Press.

entries.label: Science Communication and the Deficit Model
entries.hint: The deficit model of science communication holds that public distrust of science results from ignorance — a deficit of factual knowledge — and that correcting this deficit will produce appropriate uptake of scientific consensus. Decades of evidence have overturned this model: attitude change on climate, vaccines, and GMOs is driven more by values, identity, and trust in institutions than by information alone. The epistemological lesson is that testimony transmission and epistemic injustice are not just about individual credibility — they are structural features of how communities receive and reject expert knowledge.
entries.note: Bodmer, W. F. (1985). The Public Understanding of Science. Royal Society — the influential report that codified the deficit model. Wynne, B. (1992). 'Misunderstood Misunderstanding: Social Identities and Public Uptake of Science.' Public Understanding of Science, 1, 281-304 — the critique. Kahan, D. M. et al. (2012). 'The Polarizing Impact of Science Literacy and Numeracy on Perceived Climate Change Risks.' Nature Climate Change, 2, 732-735 — more science literacy correlates with more polarisation on politically charged topics. Oreskes, N. (2019). Why Trust Science? Princeton University Press — proposes consensus and community trust as the appropriate grounds for accepting expert testimony.

entries.label: Misinformation, Fake News, and Epistemic Infrastructure
entries.hint: The digital epistemic environment has created new challenges for social epistemology: misinformation spreads faster than corrections, recommendation algorithms create epistemic bubbles, and the distinction between authoritative and unreliable testimony is harder to make at scale. Epistemologists have applied concepts of testimonial injustice (who gets believed), peer disagreement (when experts disagree in public), and epistemic infrastructure (what institutions produce and certify knowledge) to understand and address the problem. The challenge is not merely technical — it is about the epistemic norms that govern a community.
entries.note: Lewandowsky, S., Ecker, U. K. H. & Cook, J. (2017). 'Beyond Misinformation: Understanding and Coping with the Post-Truth Era.' Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition, 6, 353-369. Wardle, C. & Derakhshan, H. (2017). Information Disorder. Council of Europe — the 'misinformation / disinformation / malinformation' taxonomy. Vosoughi, S., Roy, D. & Aral, S. (2018). 'The Spread of True and False News Online.' Science, 359, 1146-1151 — false news spreads faster than true news on Twitter. Roozenbeek, J. et al. (2020). 'Susceptibility to Misinformation about COVID-19 Across 26 Countries.' Royal Society Open Science, 7. Goldman, A. I. & Blanchard, T. (2015). 'Social Epistemology.' Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy.

entries.label: AI and Machine Knowledge
entries.hint: Large language models produce outputs that are often true, contextually apt, and arrive via processes that were reliable during training. Does this constitute knowledge? Applying the JTB analysis: LLMs form representations (belief?), some are true, and the process is somewhat reliable (justified?). But LLMs lack propositional attitudes, cannot act on their representations, and hallucinate systematically — suggesting the belief condition fails. The extended mind thesis raises a further question: if an AI system can serve as an epistemic prosthetic, does it bear any epistemic responsibility when its outputs mislead?
entries.note: Turing, A. M. (1950). 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence.' Mind, 59, 433-460 — the original framing of machine intelligence. Brown, T. et al. (2020). 'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners.' NeurIPS 2020 — GPT-3, the first LLM demonstrating scale-driven emergent capability. Floridi, L. & Cowls, J. (2019). 'A Unified Framework of Five Principles for AI in Society.' Harvard Data Science Review, 1(1). Bender, E. M. et al. (2021). 'On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots.' FAccT 2021 — 'parroting' without meaning. Williamson's knowledge-first account may be the hardest framework to satisfy: machine systems may produce knowledge-resembling outputs while lacking the mental state of knowledge entirely.

entries.label: Evidence-Based Medicine and Clinical Epistemology
entries.hint: Evidence-based medicine (EBM) formalised a hierarchy of epistemic authority for clinical decisions: systematic reviews and randomised controlled trials (RCTs) at the top; expert opinion and case reports at the bottom. The epistemological underpinning is Bayesian: each study updates prior estimates of treatment effects, and the goal is to pool evidence to yield the most reliable posterior estimate. EBM is applied Bayesian epistemology in practice, but it also faces epistemological challenges: evidence hierarchies struggle with complex interventions, context-dependence undermines external validity, and the Duhem-Quine problem means any RCT is theory-laden.
entries.note: Evidence-Based Medicine Working Group (1992). 'Evidence-Based Medicine: A New Approach to Teaching the Practice of Medicine.' JAMA, 268(17), 2420-2425 — the founding paper. Sackett, D. L. et al. (1996). 'Evidence Based Medicine: What It Is and What It Isn't.' BMJ, 312, 71-72. Cartwright, N. (2007). 'Are RCTs the Gold Standard?' BioSocieties, 2, 11-20 — the context-dependence objection. Worrall, J. (2007). 'Why There's No Cause to Randomize.' British Journal for Philosophy of Science, 58, 451-488 — philosophical critique of the RCT hierarchy. Tonelli, M. R. (1998). 'The Philosophical Limits of Evidence-Based Medicine.' Academic Medicine, 73, 1234-1240.

entries.label: Education as Epistemic Transmission
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Education is fundamentally an epistemic enterprise: it transmits knowledge, skills, and epistemic virtues across generations. Epistemological questions pervade it: what distinguishes genuine understanding from rote memorisation (knowing-that vs. knowing-how, co3)? Should education transmit knowledge or cultivate epistemic virtues? When testimony is the primary vehicle of transmission, what makes it reliable? Fricker's hermeneutical injustice is directly educational: students who lack the conceptual resources to articulate their experiences are epistemically disadvantaged from the start.
entries.note: Dewey, J. (1916). Democracy and Education. Macmillan — the foundational text connecting knowledge, experience, and democratic education. Vygotsky, L. S. (1978). Mind in Society. Harvard University Press — the zone of proximal development as a social account of knowledge transmission. Siegel, H. (1988). Educating Reason: Rationality, Critical Thinking, and Education. Routledge connects epistemic virtues to pedagogy. Zagzebski, L. (1996). Virtues of the Mind. Cambridge University Press — intellectual virtues as educational goals. Kotzee, B. (Ed.) (2013). Education and the Growth of Knowledge. Wiley-Blackwell — the standard collection on epistemology of education.

entries.label: Intellectual Property and Knowledge Ownership
entries.hint: Intellectual property law assumes that knowledge can be owned, bounded, and traded. This presupposes a particular (largely Western) epistemology: knowledge as propositional, codifiable, separable from its context of production. The TRIPS Agreement (1994) extended IP law globally, raising deep epistemological questions: can traditional knowledge (held communally, transmitted orally, embodied in practice) be protected by frameworks designed for individual propositional innovation? Whose epistemic norms govern the boundary between 'knowledge' and 'trade secret,' and who benefits from that governance?
entries.note: TRIPS Agreement (1994). Agreement on Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights. WTO. Dutfield, G. (2004). Intellectual Property, Biogenetic Resources and Traditional Knowledge. Earthscan — the standard reference on traditional knowledge and IP. Posey, D. A. (Ed.) (1999). Cultural and Spiritual Values of Biodiversity. UNEP — case studies of knowledge claims in biodiversity contexts. Shiva, V. (1997). Biopiracy: The Plunder of Nature and Knowledge. South End Press — the critical perspective. Fricker's hermeneutical injustice applies directly: traditional knowledge holders often lack access to the conceptual frameworks (patent law, trade secret doctrine) needed to articulate and protect their claims.
